title: Context Windows & Memory
eli8:
# How Much Can AI Hold in Its Head? 🧠

When you're reading a mystery novel, you can remember important clues from earlier in the story. But if the book is *really* long, you might forget what was on page 1 by the time you reach page 500!

AI models have the same challenge — they can only "remember" a limited amount of text at once.

## The Context Window 📖

The **context window** is like the AI's short-term memory — everything it can see and think about at once:

- 📝 Your question
- 💬 The whole conversation so far
- 📄 Any documents you shared
- 🤖 The AI's previous answers

All of this has to fit inside the context window! If it's too long... the early parts get *forgotten*.

## How Big Are Context Windows?

| AI Model | Context Window | Like reading... |
|----------|---------------|------------------|
| GPT-3.5 | 4K tokens | A short essay |
| GPT-4o | 128K tokens | A short novel |
| Claude 3.5 | 200K tokens | A full novel |
| Gemini 1.5 | 1M tokens | 10 novels! |

## Three Types of Memory 🗂️

AI systems actually have three different kinds of memory:

- **Working memory** (context window): What it's actively thinking about right now
- **Trained memory** (model weights): Facts it learned during training — baked into the brain!
- **External memory** (databases): Looking things up in a file or database, like using a search engine

Fun fact: Bigger context windows are *expensive* — processing 1M tokens costs much more than processing 1K tokens. It's like paying for a bigger whiteboard! 💰
detail:
# Context Windows & Memory

## What Is the Context Window?

The context window (also called **context length** or **sequence length**) is the maximum number of tokens a model can process in a single forward pass. It includes all input tokens (system prompt, conversation history, documents) plus all output tokens generated so far.

**Evolution of context lengths:**

| Model | Year | Context Length |
|-------|------|----------------|
| GPT-2 | 2019 | 1,024 tokens |
| GPT-3 | 2020 | 4,096 tokens |
| GPT-4 | 2023 | 8K → 128K tokens |
| Claude 3.5 Sonnet | 2024 | 200K tokens |
| Gemini 1.5 Pro | 2024 | 1M tokens |
| Gemini 1.5 Flash | 2024 | 1M tokens |

## Computational Cost of Long Contexts

Standard self-attention has **O(n²) time and memory complexity** in sequence length:
- Doubling the context length → 4× the attention compute
- 128K context → 128× more attention compute than 8K context

This makes long-context inference expensive. Flash Attention (O(n) memory with tiled computation) and other efficient attention variants are critical for practical long-context deployment.

## The Lost-in-the-Middle Problem

Liu et al. (2023) demonstrated that LLM performance on long-context tasks degrades significantly for information placed in the **middle** of the context window:

- Models attend most strongly to text at the **beginning** (primacy effect) and **end** (recency effect) of the context
- Information buried in the middle is recalled less reliably
- Performance on needle-in-a-haystack (NIAH) tests shows this U-shaped curve

Implication: When building RAG or long-context applications, place the most critical information at the start or end of the context, not the middle.

## Types of Memory in LLM Systems

**1. In-context memory (working memory)**
- Everything currently in the context window
- Temporary — lost when the conversation ends
- Fast and precise — exact verbatim recall
- Limited by context window size and cost

**2. Parametric memory (trained weights)**
- Knowledge encoded in the model's 7B–405B parameters during training
- Permanent — unchangeable without fine-tuning
- Fuzzy — models confabulate (hallucinate) when probed on specific facts
- Cannot be updated with new information without retraining

**3. External memory (RAG / databases)**
- Documents, structured databases, vector stores
- Retrieved at query time and injected into context
- Updatable — can add new documents without touching model weights
- Limited by retrieval quality and context window capacity

## Long-Context Evaluation Benchmarks

**RULER** (What's the Real Limit of Long-Context Language Models?): Tests retrieval, multi-hop reasoning, and aggregation over long contexts. Most models degrade significantly beyond 32K tokens.

**NIAH (Needle in a Haystack)**: Hides a specific fact ("the password is 12345") at various positions within long filler text, tests whether the model can retrieve it. Reveals position-dependent recall failures.

## Sliding Window Attention

For very long sequences, some models use **sliding window attention**: each token only attends to a local window of W preceding tokens (e.g., W=4096) rather than the full sequence. Reduces attention from O(n²) to O(n·W).

Mistral uses sliding window attention (window=4096) combined with rolling buffer KV cache. The trade-off: long-range dependencies beyond the window are not captured directly (though multi-layer stacking extends the effective receptive field).

## Token Cost Implications

Context length directly determines API cost. For Claude 3.5 Sonnet at $3/1M input tokens:
- 1,000 token query: $0.003
- 100K token context (large document): $0.30
- 200K token context (max): $0.60 per query

For high-volume applications, context window management (summarization, selective retrieval, context compression) is a meaningful cost optimization lever.

mcqs.question: What does 'context window' mean in the context of LLMs?
options: The range of topics an LLM can discuss
The maximum number of tokens the model can process in a single forward pass, including both input and output
The time period during which the model can remember previous conversations
The number of documents a RAG system retrieves
mcqs.explanation: The context window is the maximum number of tokens (input + output) an LLM can process in one forward pass. Everything outside this window is invisible to the model — it has no access to earlier parts of a conversation or document that have been truncated.

mcqs.question: Why does standard self-attention become expensive for long contexts?
options: Longer contexts require larger vocabulary sizes
Standard self-attention has O(n²) time and memory complexity in sequence length, so doubling context length quadruples compute
Long contexts exceed the precision limit of floating-point arithmetic
The tokenizer slows down significantly for long inputs
mcqs.explanation: In standard attention, each token attends to every other token, producing an n×n attention matrix. This scales quadratically: a 128K context requires 256× more attention compute than a 8K context, making long-context inference very expensive without algorithmic optimizations.

mcqs.question: What is the 'lost-in-the-middle' problem?
options: Models forget the beginning of training data over time
LLMs tend to recall information from the beginning and end of the context more reliably than information from the middle
Tokens in the middle of a sentence receive lower attention weights during tokenization
The model's performance degrades when the context window is exactly half full
mcqs.explanation: Research shows LLM performance follows a U-shaped curve over context position: information at the start (primacy) and end (recency) is recalled most reliably, while information buried in the middle is frequently missed or confabulated.

mcqs.question: What is parametric memory in an LLM?
options: A database of facts the model queries at inference time
Knowledge encoded in the model's weights during pre-training — baked into the model and unchangeable without retraining
The conversation history stored in the context window
Cached KV states from previous inference runs
mcqs.explanation: Parametric memory is the knowledge implicitly stored in the model's billions of parameters through pre-training. It's always available but is fixed (cannot be updated without retraining), and models sometimes produce confident but incorrect outputs (hallucinations) from this store.

mcqs.question: What is the Needle-in-a-Haystack (NIAH) benchmark designed to test?
options: How well a model finds synonyms in a thesaurus
Whether a model can retrieve a specific piece of information hidden at various positions within a long context
The model's ability to handle code with deeply nested functions
Multilingual understanding by hiding tokens in different languages
mcqs.explanation: NIAH hides a specific fact (the 'needle') at various positions within irrelevant filler text (the 'haystack') and tests whether the model retrieves it. It reveals position-dependent recall failures, especially the lost-in-the-middle phenomenon.

mcqs.question: Claude 3.5 Sonnet supports a context window of approximately:
options: 32K tokens
64K tokens
128K tokens
200K tokens
mcqs.explanation: Claude 3.5 Sonnet has a 200K token context window — one of the largest among major commercial models. For reference, 200K tokens is roughly 150,000 words, equivalent to a full-length novel.

flashcards.back: The maximum number of tokens (input + output combined) that a language model can process in a single forward pass. Anything outside this window is inaccessible to the model. Modern LLMs range from 8K to 1M+ tokens.

The practical constraint imposed by the context window. When exceeded, the model either truncates earlier content or raises an error. Applications must manage token budgets carefully, especially for long documents or extended conversations.
Working memory for LLMs: everything currently in the context window. Temporary (lost when the session ends), but fast and exact — the model can precisely recall anything in-context.

Knowledge encoded in the model's weights during pre-training. Always available but fixed — cannot be updated without retraining. Models sometimes hallucinate when recalling specific facts from parametric memory.

A documented failure mode where LLMs recall information from the beginning (primacy effect) and end (recency effect) of the context more reliably than information placed in the middle of a long context.

An efficient attention variant where each token only attends to a local window of W preceding tokens rather than the full sequence. Reduces attention complexity from O(n²) to O(n·W). Used in Mistral's architecture.

A long-context evaluation benchmark that hides a specific fact at various positions within long filler text and tests whether the model can retrieve it. Reveals position-dependent recall failures in long-context models.

videos.title: Context Windows in LLMs Explained
videos.description: Clear explanation of what context windows are, why they matter, how different models compare, and the practical implications for building LLM-powered applications including cost and truncation handling.

videos.title: Intro to Large Language Models — Andrej Karpathy
videos.description: Karpathy covers the three types of memory in LLM systems (in-context, weights, external) and how context windows relate to the full LLM stack. A strong conceptual foundation for understanding memory limitations.
